segments.s589-g33: به دارو is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'by/with medicine' (به here = 'by, with'); چو یک هفته introduces a temporal clause 'when/for a week'; بستی is a past tense verb 'bound/tied/applied' (here a past action), and پزشک is the agent (subject) placed after the verb — a common poetic word order. Note that the verb ends in -ی, a poetic/classical past ending used here as a third-person past form, so the clause reads 'when the physician bound (it) with medicine for a week.'
segments.s589-g34: دگر هفته is a temporal phrase 'the next/another week'; خون is the subject 'blood'; آمدی is the past-tense verb 'came' — again with the poetic/classical -ی ending used as third-person past; چون + سرشک is a simile 'like a teardrop(s).' The whole segment means 'in the next week blood came like teardrops.'
segments.s589-g25: This is a subordinate clause introduced by که ('that') with a first-person subject من ('I') followed by the direct object phrase چشمه سو ('that spring/that source') and the negative-present form نبینم ('I do not see' / 'I may not see'). The phrase به چشم ('with the eye' / 'to the eye') is placed after the verb for poetic inversion (normal prose would be به چشم نبینم or نبینمش به چشم).
segments.s589-g26: A balanced negative coordination: نه ... نه ... = 'neither ... nor ...'. Each هنگام ('at the time of') is followed by a noun naming the occasion (شادی 'joy', خشم 'anger'), so the line means 'neither at the time of joy nor at the time of anger.'
segments.s589-g27: برین is a compound sense 'بر + این' ('upon this' / 'on this matter'); نیز ('also') adds emphasis; برگشت (here past/preterite) means 'turned' or 'was reversed' and گردون is the poetic word for 'heaven/sky' — the temporal phrase سه ماه ('three months') follows to mark duration. Word order is compact and poetic: 'On this also the heavens turned for three months.'
segments.s589-g28: زمانه ('the age' or 'the world') is the grammatical subject; به جوش آمد is an idiom ('came to a boil' = 'seethed, was in turmoil') with به + noun + آمد; از here means 'from' or 'because of' and links to خون شاه ('the king's blood') as the cause or source of the turmoil. The verb آمد is simple past/preterite narration.
segments.s589-g29: چو ('when') introduces a temporal clause. بیدادگر شد is a past-tense predicate 'became a tyrant/cruel one.' شبان ('the shepherd') is the subject; با رمه ('with the flock') is an adverbial phrase indicating accompaniment or what came with him. The order is typical for epic poetry: temporal + predicate + subject + accompaniment.
segments.s589-g30: بدو is the clitic combination به + او ('to him'); بازگردد is used as a past/perfective narrative 'returned' or 'were turned back' (classical Persian often uses present forms in narration), and بدیها همه ('all the evils') is the object subject of that returning. The literal order is 'to him returned all the evils.'
segments.s589-g31: ز ('from') marks origin; بینیش is a possessive form (بین + ـش) meaning 'his sight/eye/nose' in context — here 'from his eye(s)'; بگشاد is a classical poetic perfect/past 'opened' or 'burst open' (the sense is 'opening/releasing'); یک روز ('one day') gives the time; خون ('blood') is the thing that 'opened' out. Word order places the verb-form בگشاد before the temporal phrase for emphasis.
segments.s589-g32: پزشک آمد is straightforward past narration 'a physician came'; از هر سوی ('from every direction/quarter') is the source phrase, with سوی here meaning 'direction/side'; رهنمون ('guide' or 'guidance') likely describes the physicians' role (they came as guides/attendants). The clause is a compact report of people arriving to help.
segments.s589-g9: This clause is a rhetorical question. که introduces the clause ('that'/'for'), تا کی is an idiom meaning 'how long' (تا 'until' + کی 'when'), بود is the copula 'be' placed before the locative phrase در جهان ('in the world'), and مرگ اوی is a possessive phrase 'his death' (اوی = his). Word order is typical older Persian with the verb before the locative complement.
segments.s589-g10: This is a second interrogative clause coordinated to the first. کجا ('where') begins the question; تیره گردد literally 'becomes dark/bleak' uses گردد (an older subjunctive/optative form of 'become') placed after the adjective; سر و ترگ ('head and garment/tatter') is a compound of two nouns joined by و, and اوی again marks possession 'his'. The verb/auxiliary follows the adjectives as in classical Persian.
segments.s589-g11: This is an exclamatory/interrogative framing: چه باشد کجا باشد ('what will it be, where will it be'). باشد is a classical subjunctive/optative form of 'to be' used here to ask about possibility/existence; آن روزگار ('that time/day') is the noun being questioned. The repetition of باشد gives a heightened, poetic sense.
segments.s589-g12: This relative clause introduced by که ('that') gives the condition being asked about. پژمرده 'withered' is a predicate adjective, and گردد (from گشت/گرد) is the verbal 'become' placed before the noun گل ('flower'); شهریار 'sovereign/king' is the possessor of گل. The structure is 'that (time) when the sovereign's flower becomes withered.'
segments.s589-g13: ستاره‌شمر 'the astrologer' is the subject and گفت is the past reporting verb 'said'. کاین خود مباد is an emphatic optative formula: کاین ('that this') + خود (intensifier 'itself/very') + مباد (an old negative optative meaning 'may it not be' or 'may it not happen'). The whole segment reports a prayer or wish against the event.
segments.s589-g14: This is the content clause introduced by که ('that'). شاه جهان 'the king of the world' is the noun phrase; گیرد is present/subjunctive of 'take/seize' used transitively here with یاد ('remembrance') as object introduced by از ('of/from' meaning 'of'). Literally 'that the king take remembrance of death' → 'that the king be reminded of death.'
segments.s589-g15: چو ('when/if') introduces a temporal/conditional clause. بخت شهنشاه ('the emperor's fate/fortune') is the subject phrase; بدرو is an adjective meaning 'ill‑disposed/ill‑omened' applied to بخت, and شود is the verbal 'become' placed at the end. The clause reads 'when the emperor's fortune becomes ill.'
segments.s589-g16: This clause states the consequence: از ایدر ('from here') marks origin, سوی چشمه سو ('toward the spring') gives direction—سوی ... سو is a doubled directional phrasing (both words mean 'toward/side'), and شود again is the verb 'become'/'turn'. The subject is understood from the previous clause (the fortune/destiny).
segments.s589-g17: فراز is an adverbial/adv phrase meaning 'on high' or 'up'; آورد is past tense 'brought/raised' with an implicit subject; the following list (لشکر و بوق و کوس) is the direct object: 'the army and the horn(s) and the drum(s)'. The conjunction و simply links the list items.
segments.s589-g18: به شادی is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in/with joy' (here 'by/-ly' sense of به); نظاره (literally 'viewing, gaze') plus شود (an impersonal/passive-use of 'to become/happen') means 'there was gazing' or more naturally 'eyes/gaze were turned toward'; سوی طوس marks the direction/target 'toward Tūs'.
segments.s589-g19: بر آن جایگه is a locative phrase 'on/at that place'; the second بر + بود (archaic word order) yields 'was upon'—i.e. his attention/sense was fixed there; هوش اوی is 'his awareness' with اوی as the enclitic possessive 'his'.
segments.s589-g20: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; این راز بگذشت بر گوش اوی is an idiomatic phrase: 'this secret passed to his ear' meaning 'he heard this secret'. بگذشت is past 'passed/reached', and بر گوش اوی = 'upon his ear' (i.e. he heard it).
segments.s589-g21: ازین is 'from this' (از + این fused); دانَش = 'knowledge'; ار is the conditional 'if'; یادگیری is 'learning' (object of the conditional); به دست is the idiomatic instrument phrase 'by hand / by means of' (here 'if you can obtain/learn by means of this'). The clause is elliptical: 'if you can learn from this knowledge by means of (your) effort...'
segments.s589-g22: که introduces the subordinate explanatory clause 'for/that'; این راز در پرده = 'this secret in a veil/hidden'; ایزدست is a predicate meaning roughly 'is by God's will' or 'is in God's keeping'—i.e. the secret is hidden/kept by God.
segments.s589-g23: چو = 'when' (temporal); بشنید زو = 'he heard of it' (بشنید past 'heard', زو = 'of it/from it'); شاه سوگند خورد = 'the king swore an oath' where سوگند خوردن is an idiom 'to take/swear an oath' (خورد is the light verb used with سوگند).
segments.s589-g24: به here functions as an instrumental/prepositional 'by' (swearing by something); خراد برزین is a titular/name phrase — خراد as an epithet 'wise' modifying the name برزین (Barzin); و links the two items sworn by; خورشید زرد = 'the yellow/pale sun' (a poetic oath-formula).
segments.s589-g1: وزان پس is an adverbial phrase meaning 'from that time on' (وزان = 'from that time', پس = 'after/then'). غم و شادی یزدگرد is a possessed noun phrase: the possessed items 'sorrow and joy' come before the possessor 'Yazdegerd', so literally 'Yazdegerd's sorrow and joy'.
segments.s589-g2: چنان گشت is a past/resultative phrase 'became thus' (گشت = 'became'). بر پور is a prepositional phrase 'upon/against the son' (بر + پور). چون introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by باد 'wind' used as the vehicle of the simile; ارد here functions as a proper/name element in the simile (not a common noun meaning 'flour').
segments.s589-g3: برین = بر + این ('upon/after this'); نیز 'also' / 'moreover'. چندی زمان is a time-quantifier 'some time' or 'for a while', and برگذشت is the past verb 'passed by' or 'elapsed' (verb at the end as usual in Persian).
segments.s589-g4: به ایران is a prepositional phrase 'to/into Iran' (here rendered 'in/into Iran'), followed by the noun phrase پدر پور فرخ which groups 'the father (pader) of Pour‑Farrokh' or the father named Pourfarrokh (the exact name/epithet order is idiomatic). The final به دشت indicates motion/tendency 'into the plain' ('به' + place noun).
segments.s589-g5: ز شاهی marks the topic/cause 'concerning kingship' (ز = 'about/from', شاهی = 'kingship'). پراندیشه شد is a compound meaning 'became full of thought / pensive' (پراندیشه = 'full of thought', شد = past 'became'). The subject یزدگرد follows the predicate, a frequent poetic order.
segments.s589-g6: ز هر کشوری is a distributive/locative phrase 'throughout/from every country'. موبدان کرد گرد is a causative/resultative cluster: کرد 'made/caused' + گرد (here verbal 'to go about'), so together 'he caused the mōbeds to go about/to be sent' — i.e., he sent priests to every land.
segments.s589-g7: به اخترشناسان is the indirect object 'to/for the astrologers' placed before the verb. بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb meaning 'he commanded/said', and شاه (the subject) is placed after the verb for emphasis/honorific order.
segments.s589-g8: که introduces an object clause 'that...'; تا here expresses purpose/optative 'so that/in order that'. کردهر یک is a proper name or title ('Kordhar' + 'one/each'), and به اختر نگاه is the verb phrase 'look toward the star(s)' (به + اختر = 'to the stars', نگاه = 'look/observe'), with the final noun-verb order typical of Persian subordinate clauses.

sentences.s589-s17: When the physician bound it with medicine for a week, in the following week blood came like teardrops.
sentences.s589-s13: that I should not see that spring with my eye — neither in times of joy nor in times of anger.
sentences.s589-s14: Upon this the heavens also turned; for three months the age seethed with the king's blood.
sentences.s589-s15: When the shepherd became a tyrant, all evils returned to him with the flock.
sentences.s589-s16: One day blood gushed from his eye(s); physicians came from every quarter as guides.
sentences.s589-s5: How long will his death linger in the world, and where will his head and tattered garment grow dark?
sentences.s589-s6: What will that time be, and where will that day be when the sovereign's flower withers?
sentences.s589-s7: The astrologer said, 'May this not happen — that the king of the world be made to remember death.'
sentences.s589-s8: When the emperor's fortune turns bad, it will turn from here toward the spring.
sentences.s589-s9: He raised up the army, trumpets and drums; in joy the gaze was turned toward Tūs.
sentences.s589-s10: At that place his awareness was upon him; when this secret passed to his ear.
sentences.s589-s11: If from this knowledge you can gain learning by means of (your) hand, for this secret is in God's veil.
sentences.s589-s12: When the king heard of it, he swore an oath by (the) wise Barzin and the yellow sun.
sentences.s589-s1: From that time on, Yazdegerd's sorrow and joy fell upon his son, as if like a wind of Ard.
sentences.s589-s2: Some more time passed; in Iran the father of Pourfarrokh went out into the plain.
sentences.s589-s3: Yazdegerd became full of thought about kingship; he caused mōbeds to go to every country.
sentences.s589-s4: The king commanded the astrologers that each Kordhar should observe the stars.
